creator: madpilot@FreeBSD.org
text:
(In reply to Daniel Engberg from comment #3)

If removing cddb from the tree is a decision that has already been taken (has there been any public discussion about this?), I will try to adapt the audacious port. Since upstream does not provide a simple toggle, I will need to implement it, could take some time.

I don't care for cddb, so I have no problems with the decision itself.

I do have multiple issues with the reasoning and methodology you give for the decision though. Since I don't care no problem at present, but I would not like such reasoning/methodology applied to software/libraries I use and care about. (see below if you are interested in details)

Since removing support for cddb from audacious requires some work on meson files (about which I'm no expert) I will need some time, but I think to be able to do it in less than a month.

--- Rant mode ---

Some details:

- "it is legacy", so are pens and paper, but there is no hurry to get rid of them (since they are so useful/available, don't depend on electrical power for using etc.). The definition of being legacy is not well defined, and anyway the conclusion that something should be removed does not follow automatically from it. It is a factory, but many other factors should be accounted for.

- You say broken but do not specify what is being broken. The library has no breakage, the protocol works fine. What is being periodically broken are servers (which are expensive to maintain, I guess). But servers ARE available, up and running. There are alternative ones, the default ones require registration, which, I agree, is unfortunate, but registering is an option. A lot of online services with open source clients require some kind of registration.

- The fact that other and better protocols/libraries is a good argument as long as support for those is readily available, in this case (and many similar cases) it is not, so I don't see it as a good justification.

- "other distros" is just like when I was a kid and told my parents "all my friends are doing it"... Every country has its own variation of the stock parent reply but tit usually goes something like "if all your friends were jumping off a bridge, would you jump too?", which is not really a good counterpoint. There may be very good reasons for going with the flow, but as the "legacy" point, it is a factor, but definitely not a decisive one.

Read this like "maybe removing CDDB completely IS a good idea, but you did not prove it satisfactorily to me, but since I don't care, no objection from me; in fact, let me help pour the gasoline." :)

Please bear in mind I do not care for cddb and am not advocating it being conserved, I will not fight such a battle, but the reasons given are not good ones, and if I cared for it, or the same reasons were used to justify removing something I use I would not be happy.

Is maintaining libcddb in the ports such a big burden? Is the library itself vulnerable?

Do we care ass the FreeBSD project if the protocol keeps existing and servers (with mandatory registration or not) keep existing? In other words, has the FreeBSD project any interest in crusading against CDDB itself?

--- end rant mode ---
